skill.name: chemical-structure-converter
skill.description: Convert between IUPAC names, SMILES strings, molecular formulas, and common names for chemical compounds. Supports SMILES validation, batch processing, structure standardization, and cheminformatics database preparation for drug discovery workflows.
skill.summary: Interconvert chemical identifiers including IUPAC names, SMILES strings, and molecular formulas for cheminformatics workflows.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 74 static analysis findings were evaluated and determined to be false positives. The skill is a benign chemical structure converter with a hardcoded local compound database and no runtime network calls, file system writes, or command execution. The 41 'shell backtick execution' findings are CLI usage examples in markdown code blocks. The 11 'weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are from YAML frontmatter markers and markdown horizontal rules mistaken for PEM boundaries. All hardcoded URLs are legitimate scientific database references (PubChem, ChemSpider, RDKit). The 2 heuristic combination alerts were based on these individual false positives. No credential access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection patterns exist.

low_findings.title: Shell backtick patterns in documentation code blocks
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 41 instances of backtick execution patterns in SKILL.md. All occurrences are inside markdown code blocks (bash and python) showing CLI usage examples and workflow commands. These are documentation examples, not runtime command execution. No eval(), exec(), subprocess, or os.system calls exist in the actual Python script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All 41 flagged locations are within markdown fenced code blocks presenting documentation examples. The actual Python file (scripts/main.py) contains no subprocess or shell execution calls.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm markers from markdown formatting
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 11 instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' across SKILL.md and scripts/main.py. The SKILL.md matches are from YAML frontmatter '---' markers and markdown horizontal rules that resemble PEM boundary markers. The scripts/main.py match at line 96 is an argparse constructor call. No cryptographic operations of any kind exist in this skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No MD5, SHA1, AES, DES, RSA, or any other cryptographic algorithm is used or referenced in any file. The scanner confused YAML frontmatter and markdown separators with PEM boundaries.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs are legitimate reference links
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 10 URLs as 'hardcoded URL' findings. These are all documentation references: the GitHub source repository link, PubChem database URLs for compound lookup, ChemSpider search URLs, the OpenSMILES specification, and RDKit documentation. No credentials, API keys, or endpoints to unknown or suspicious domains are present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All URLs point to well-known scientific databases (PubChem, ChemSpider) and open-source documentation (OpenSMILES, RDKit). No obfuscation, dynamic URL construction, or suspicious domains detected.

low_findings.title: C2 keywords match compound names in chemical database
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'caffeine' and 'ethanol' at scripts/main.py lines 23 and 36 as C2 keywords. These are compound names in a hardcoded chemical compound dictionary used for structure lookups. The skill is a chemical structure converter, and these are legitimate entries in the compound database.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged words are dictionary keys for chemical compounds (caffeine C8H10N4O2, ethanol C2H6O) in a COMPOUND_DB data structure. No network communication or command-and-control behavior is present.

low_findings.title: Certificate and key file false positive on print statement
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged scripts/main.py line 111 for 'Certificate/key files'. This line is a print statement iterating over compound names in a database: 'for name in converter.COMPOUND_DB.keys()'. No certificate files, PEM files, key stores, or credential material exists in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The word 'keys' in .keys() method was likely matched by the scanner. It is a Python dictionary method call returning compound names, not cryptographic key material.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance false positive on SMILES validation
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 3 instances of 'system reconnaissance' and 'network reconnaissance' in SKILL.md and scripts/main.py. These include SMILES syntax validation string comparisons and a capability description. No system enumeration, port scanning, network mapping, or information gathering behavior exists.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The SMILES validation function counts parentheses and brackets in strings, and the SKILL.md line is a capability description. Neither involves system or network reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Path traversal reference in audit recommendation
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged chemical-structure-converter_audit_result_v4.json line 233 for 'Path traversal sequence'. The line contains text within an audit recommendation suggesting that path traversal protection should be added. It is documentation about a potential future improvement, not actual path traversal code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: This is text in a JSON audit report recommending path traversal protection be added. No file I/O operations exist in scripts/main.py. No path manipulation or directory traversal logic exists in any skill file.

content.value_statement: Chemical researchers spend significant time manually converting between IUPAC names, SMILES strings, and molecular formulas. This skill automates chemical identifier conversion with structured output, SMILES validation, and batch processing support for faster cheminformatics workflows.
actual_capabilities: Convert common compound names to SMILES strings, IUPAC names, molecular formulas, and molecular weight
Validate SMILES syntax including parenthesis matching and bracket balance
Convert SMILES strings to IUPAC names using a local compound database
Convert IUPAC names to SMILES strings
Provide structured output with all available chemical identifiers for a given compound
Batch process multiple compounds and report conversion success and failure counts
limitations: Local database contains only common compounds such as aspirin, caffeine, glucose, and ethanol
PubChem API fallback is documented but not yet implemented in the Python script
SMILES validation is limited to syntax checks and does not verify chemical validity
Cannot generate 2D or 3D molecular structures from chemical identifiers

use_cases.title: Standardize compound identifiers for drug discovery databases
use_cases.description: A medicinal chemist preparing a compound library needs consistent SMILES and IUPAC representations for all entries. This skill converts names to standardized identifiers and validates SMILES syntax before database insertion.

use_cases.title: Teach chemical notation to students
use_cases.description: An educator teaching organic chemistry uses this skill to show how IUPAC names map to SMILES strings and molecular formulas. Students learn the relationships between different chemical representations.

use_cases.title: Identify unknown compounds from literature references
use_cases.description: A lab researcher encounters an unfamiliar compound name in a paper and uses this skill to retrieve its SMILES string and molecular formula for further analysis or synthesis planning.

prompt_templates.title: Convert a compound name to its chemical identifiers
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert aspirin to its chemical identifiers. Show the SMILES string, IUPAC name, molecular formula, and molecular weight.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick lookup of a known compound's chemical data

prompt_templates.title: Validate a SMILES string for syntax errors
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate the SMILES string C1CC and explain what is wrong with it. Then suggest the corrected SMILES and show the compound it represents.
prompt_templates.scenario: Debugging a malformed SMILES string before database entry

prompt_templates.title: Batch convert multiple compounds
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert the following compounds to their chemical identifiers: aspirin, caffeine, glucose, ethanol. For each compound show the SMILES, IUPAC name, and molecular formula. Report how many were converted successfully.
prompt_templates.scenario: Processing a list of compounds for database standardization

prompt_templates.title: Handle an unknown compound not in the database
prompt_templates.prompt: Look up the chemical identifiers for ritonavir. If the compound is not in the local database, explain what happened and provide alternative lookup URLs for verification.
prompt_templates.scenario: Working with novel or less common compounds outside the built-in database

output_examples.input: Show me the SMILES and molecular formula for caffeine.
output_examples.output: The skill returns a structured table with caffeine's IUPAC name (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine), SMILES string (CN1C=NC2=C1C(=O)N(C(=O)N2C)C), molecular formula (C8H10N4O2), and molecular weight (194.19 g/mol). All values are extracted from the local compound database.

The skill identifies that the SMILES string C1CC has a ring closure error. The ring opened with digit '1' is not closed with another '1'. It explains that the corrected SMILES should include a second '1' to close the ring, like C1CC1 (cyclopropane).

The skill flags that ritonavir is not found in the local database (which contains only common compounds like aspirin, caffeine, glucose, and ethanol). It provides a direct PubChem lookup URL and a ChemSpider search URL for the user to find the compound information externally.

best_practices: Use complete and specific compound names to avoid ambiguous matches in the database. For example, use 'acetaminophen' rather than 'APAP' or 'paracetamol'.
Cross-reference critical compound data against PubChem before using it in production drug discovery workflows, since the local database is limited.
Validate all SMILES strings with this skill before inserting them into a compound database to catch syntax errors early.
anti_patterns: Do not fabricate SMILES strings or molecular weights for compounds not found in the database. Flag unknown compounds and use external lookup instead.
Do not use this skill for biological activity prediction, molecular docking, or spectroscopic interpretation. It only converts chemical identifiers.
Do not rely on the local database for proprietary or proprietary compounds. The database contains only four common compounds for demonstration.

faq.question: What chemical formats can this skill convert?
faq.answer: This skill converts between IUPAC names, SMILES strings, molecular formulas, and common names for chemical compounds.

faq.question: How many compounds are in the local database?
faq.answer: The local database contains four common compounds: aspirin, caffeine, glucose, and ethanol. For other compounds, use the PubChem or ChemSpider lookup URLs provided by the skill.

faq.question: Can this skill generate 3D molecular structures?
faq.answer: No. This skill converts chemical identifiers only. It cannot generate 2D or 3D molecular structures or perform molecular modeling.

faq.question: Does the skill validate SMILES strings?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill checks SMILES syntax including matching parentheses, square brackets, and ring closure digits. Validation is syntax-based and does not verify chemical correctness.

faq.question: Can I process multiple compounds at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports batch processing of multiple compound names. It converts each one and provides a summary of how many were converted successfully.

faq.question: What should I do if a compound is not in the database?
faq.answer: The skill flags the compound as not found and provides direct lookup URLs for PubChem and ChemSpider where you can find the missing data.
